SALT LAKE CITY,	JUNE 15, 1892.


THIS, THAT, AND THE OTHER.

	The national editorial party has left the Pacific
Coast, some of them going in one direction and
some in another; a part of the excursion go to
Salt Lake City; some of them via Portland and
the Northern country, and others, in a more
easterly direction, expecting to take in the various
sights and wonders, and look up the Great West
generally.
	Here in San Francisco the weather is pleasant
and the air delightful, with the fragrance of buds
and blossoms.  One can scarcely express the ad-
miration awakened by the floral beauties of this
garden of the world.  It is necessary to see for
one's self in order to believe, for no description
given can do justice to the subject.  Poets may
write, and artists may paint in glowing colors, but
"seeing is believing," for none of these however
clever can approach the reality.
	After going out to the beach and spending a
day there, which is the only relaxation we can
take time to indulge in, then we will turn home-
ward not from choice, for there is much still to
be seen that would be very desirable, only editors
must be at their post of duty and not away float-
ing with the tide of pleasure, or of circumstances.
One gets much material for use even in a short
trip to this Coast, for there is so much that is
new and suggestive and the newspapers, if one
can but take the time to glance through them, O,
dear,—the startling newsey news.
	The Pacific Coast Woman's Press Association
is what interests us most of all here, and the
ladies are doing good work in many ways.  The
President' Mrs. Nellie Blessing Eyster, the new
Cor. Sec'y Mrs. Hannah Neall, the Recording
Sec'y. Mrs. F. W. D'Eveyn, and the Treasurer
Mrs. Florence Percy Mathesen with the Ex. Com.
all seem to be very efficient and full of hope, as
well as abounding in energy, and with such able
women as Mrs. Alice Carey Waterman and Miss
Minna V. Lewis, Miss Eliza D. Keith (Di Vernon)
and others associated in their work, they must
make a success of any laudable undertaking.
They are warm-hearted, generous, and hospitable,
clever, witty and amiable. Mrs. Mathesen was a
Maine girl and talks of her native state enthusi-
astically.  She is arranging to issue very soon a
volume of poems of Pacific Coast writers, which
promises to be an interesting work.  The lady is
a poet herself as well as a story writer and writes
for Phila. Saturday Evening Post, Leslie's Illus-
trated Weekly, Portland Transcript, Wide Awake,
Metropolitan and other journals.
	Miss Lewis is a correspondent of the Business
Woman's Journal, N. Y. Newspaperdom, Kate
Field's Washington, Harper's Bazar, Californian
Illustrated, and the Home Maker. Mrs. D'Evelyn
(Kate Mc'Bride) writes for the Victoria College
Magazine, and Chicago Womad's News. Mrs.


Emily Browne Powell writes for the N. Y. Tri-
bune, Boston American Union, Ladies Home
Journal, of Phila., Springfield Daily Union,
Californian Illustrated, and local papers.  Mrs.
Waterman keeps her own counsel in regard to
her newspaper work and we have not yet ascer-
tained what papers she contributes to, but she is
a wide awake, earnest young woman, very inter-
esting in conversation.  Mrs. Maggie Downing
Brainard, of San Jose, is a typical Southern wo-
man, reminds one very much of Mrs. Willmirth
East, is from Mississippi and decidedly clever,
she is Ass’t editor of Santa Clara Valley Monthly,
correspondent to Clarion Ledger, Raymond Gazette
and Natchez Banner, (all of Mississippi.
	San Francisco, May 27, 1892.


[Column 1]

﻿THE MEN AND WOMEN WE MET.

IN SAN FRANCISCO.

	On the afternoon of Tuesday May 24, Dr. Alice
B. Stockham and her daughter Miss Cora Stock-
ham tendered a reception to the ladies engaged
in the Kindergarten work, the Pacific Coast Wo-
man's Press Association, and the visiting ladies
of the editorial party at the parlors of the Palace
Hotel. Miss Stockham is a lovely young woman,
her very presence is like an inspiration and one
can readily believe what Mrs. Stockham has
stated that her daughter was her inspiration in
preparing her work on Tokology. This valuable
work has been widely circulated in Utah; and
is the most complete work of the kind we have
ever examined.
	We had the pleasure of going to the reception
with Mrs. Waterman, Miss Lewis, Mrs, D'Evelyn,
and Mrs. Mathesen of the P. C. W. P. A. Miss
Stockham received the ladies in the most graceful
and charming way, though her mother had not yet
returned from her trip around the bay with the
editorial party, but came in later, looking very
weary, though in excellent spirits and full of ani-
mation. Mrs. Eyster, of San Francisco, Mrs.
Sarah Wilder Pratt, of Chicago were with her,
also some others; Mrs. Pratt is not only a writer
for the press, but is an ordained minister of
Christian Science and fills several important
positions in Women's Clubs etc., all the ladies
were rather tired as they had been traveling so long
and seeing so much.
	Dr. Stockham had just returned from a trip to
Japan and of course every one was anxious to
hear of that wonderful far-off country; she has
brought with her many interesting curiosities, as
well as a great deal of information, which she will
disseminate for the benefit of the women of her
country, as she is a very pleasing and interesting
writer and story teller.
	The same evening the Convention of the
National Editorial Association opened in the
Metropolitan Hall opposite the U. S. Mint build-
ing. A very fine looking audience assembled,
though the larger part were the visiting delega-
tion. The Chairman of the reception committee
of the California delegation, read an address of
welcome which was very complimentary to the
visitors, and set forth in the most glowing terms
the advantages of California in every respect, its
present prospects, and its great possible and
probable future.
	The President, Mr. W. S. Capeller, made the
address of the evening which was very able and
admirably delivered. These two addresses were
the principal features of the opening meeting.
	One of the most notable men of the Conven-
tion and of the editorial association is the Cor.
Secretary, Mr. J. M. Page, of Jerseyville, Ill.,
editor of the Democrat, he has filled the office he
holds eight years in succession. He is to the N.
E. A. what Rachel Foster Avery is to the N.-A.
W. S. A. has the same soft of wonderful tact and

[Column 2]

talent to grasp the situation of affairs and to not
only make a success of what he undertakes but
to do whatever is best for the Association to the
perfect satisfaction of all concerned. To manage
a large party of men and women, look after the
transportation and see that everything goes right,
at the same time to do the secretary's work and
answer every one's questions in the most polite
and gracious manner as if it were a pleasure, is
what but few people are capable of doing.
	There might be a great deal written of the do-
ings of the Convention, but as it is quite impos-
sible to "tell it all" especially at one time in a
small paper, we will leave some things to the
imagination, and some to use at another time in
these columns,
	One of the best articles read at the Convention
was (in our estimation) "Women In Journalism"
by Sallie A. Moses, and read by Mrs. S. W. Pratt
who gave good expression to the subject, modu-
lating her voice well and altogether rendering it
in a clear and forcible style,which was very pleas-
ing to her hearers.
	On the last day of the Convention, there was a
great deal of unfinished business to be disposed
of and the officers for the ensuing year were to
be chosen, and altogether with arranging trans-
portation and bringing matters to a close there
was a good deal of a "hubbub." However the vot-
ing went on pretty well until some one suggested
a woman for third Vice President; on this ques-
tion, there were a variety of opinions. Some
members holding that Mrs. E. Jeanette Abbott a
Chicago lady, the nominee, was not eligible, and
some gentlemen were anxious to convince those
around them that it would be impossible for a
woman to preside, one man near where we were
sitting said imagine a woman presiding over such
an assembly as this?"
	Sure enough, we thought, for even the Presi-
dent, Mr. Capeller, who is trying his best to main-
tain order, and declaring aloud he would "have
harmony if he had to fight for it" could not pos-
sibly preside with dignity, but if a woman had
been seated in the chair men would have behaved
better. Miss Anthony, Miss Willard, or Mrs.
Sewall and other women we know could have
kept just as good order as Mr. Capeller with all
due deference to the gentleman, his dignity, and
self-possession and his well-trained voice.
	Some of the members were loud in their praise
of Mrs. Abbott and she had a large number of
votes, but the tide of popular opinion was in favor
of the other nominee and so he was elected. Mrs.
Abbott seemed to be very popular with the
llinois delegation and with many others. She
conducts the Elite News, and is the Assistant
Cor. Sec'y of the Illinois Woman's Press Asso-
ciation and is said to be a very charming woman
porsonally. It would have been a very graceful
thing to have elected her, and a sort of a tacit ac-
knowledgement of woman's ability. To make
some sort of reparation or as a compliment to the
ladies, Miss Louise E. Francis was nominated as
Assistant Recording Secretary, and again an ob-
jection, this time that the Constitution made no
provision for such an office, Mr. Capellar declared
her elected any way, and whether she is really a
bona fide officer or not she may know, but we are
not at all sure. Miss Frances is a very competent
young person, has a great deal of independence,
and carries on her own business as a newspaper
editor and proprietor. She is a Californian by
birth and has been educated in her own state.
Her paper is the Castroville Enterprise and is the
official organ of the Pacific Coast Woman's Press
Association. At some future time we hope to
tell our readers more about these bright, plucky
California newspaper women.
	On Manday evening, May 30, there was an in-
formal reception at Mrs. Eyster's in order to bring
together some of the ladies who had not yet left
he city and have them meet members of the P.


Walton full in the face, "I don't quite under-
stand your meaning Doctor Bland.  Do you
insinuate that I am a coward to come into my
enemy's house after his death, to call on a
matter of importance?"
	Dr. Bland did not desire to reply and Wal-
ton rode on in silence until they reached the
courtyard.  Both gentlemen dismounted and
as the Doctor was familiar with the place he
escorted Lord Walton into the house and they
were shown into the library.
	"Tell Miss Conway a gentleman wishes to
see her on business," said the Doctor to the
servant, who soon returned saying Miss Con-
way would be down in a few minutes.
	Jane Conway wondered when summoned by
the servant what had brought a fine gentleman
to see her, for the serving man had described
him as a "foine big man," and she could not
imagine who it could be.  She was not long
in ascertaining, for coming into the room she
was introduced by the doctor to Lord Walton
and she knew at once he was Maitland's father
and suspected the rest.  The interview was not
a long one, and when it was over Lord Walton
insisted upon being presented to Miss Hillard.


WOMAN'S EXPONENT.


EDITORIAL NOTES.
	This is the first time in the history of the
United States that women have been made Dele-
gates to a Presidential Convention, but Wyoming
true to the principles of her Constitution elected
one lady, Mrs. Theresa Jenkins and one alternate
Mrs. Georgianna Carlton, the last named lady
Utah has some honor in claiming as she spent
most of her life here, and all her early education 
and training she received in this Territory.
	The Conference of the Relief Society of this
stake will be held in the Salt Lake Assembly
Hall in this City Friday June 17.  Meetings will
commence as usual at 10 a. and 2 p.m. The
President is desirous of a good attendance of the
members from the wards of the city and from all
parts of the county.  As this is not the time of
year for reports it is expected the Conference will
be of an interesting character.  A cordial invita-
tion is extended to the brethren and sisters in-
terested in this benevolent institution and especi-
ally the Presidency of the Church, the Apostles,
the Presiding Bishops, the Presidency of the
Stake and the Bishops of the several wards. By 
order of the President, M. Isabella Horne.

	The terrible calamities that have been chroni-
cled in the newspapers the last few weeks are
indeed terrifying; while famine rages in one part
of the earth, and war is threatened in another,
floods, cyclones,  storms,  whirlwinds,  raging
torrents, explosions, fires, and disease and death

[column 2]
all these with the destruction of human life and
property seem almost to overwhelm one with
the sickening record; and involuntarily one con-
templates—what is and may be yet to come to
pass. The prophecies of the Scriptures are being
fulfilled in the sight of the people.  Here in these
peaceful vales there is plenty and comparative
quiet, surely there is reason to rejoice and be
grateful that all is as well as it is with this peo-
ple here in the valleys of the mountains.
	The Republican Convention at Minneapolis has
had rather a lively time; after considerable de-
bating and delay, O. J. Salisbery and Frank J.
Cannon were admitted to seats in the Convention
and also Messrs. Goodwin and Varian with each
side one vote.  While the Convention was in pro-
cess of organization, some radical changes took
place in the political atmosphere of the country,
Blaine's resignation as Secretary of State causing
some people to look upon him as almost a traitor
to the President and Cabinet—however this may
be viewed it was doubtless a disappoinment to 
many of his ardent admirers, who have heretofore
looked upon him as the greatest man of his party.
That it will somewhat lessen his influence and his
fame seems more than probable.  At present, in-
dications point to a more turbulent state of the
political affairs of the nation than there has ever
been at any presidential election.  Bitterness in
the two factions of Blaine and Harrison men is
lamentable when one considers the welfare of the 
country is at stake.  On the 10th, the dispatches
announced Harrison's nomination on the first
ballot;  also Whitelaw Reid of New York, for
Vice President; this ends the contest so far.

	The recent visit of Mrs. Solomon Thatcher of
Chicago, to Utah, was a very pleasing affair, as
well as beneficial in the interests of the Columbian
Exposition. Mrs. Thatcher is a very gifted and
brilliant woman, and intensely interested in the
woman's department of the World's Fair; of
which she is one of the Executive Committee.
She addressed the people of Logan, Cache Co.,
while there on a visit to Hon. Moses Thatcher
and family, and had a large audience in the Logan
Tabernacle. On Sunday evening she spoke in
this City, in the large Tabernacle, to the largest
congregation she has ever had the honor of ad-
dressing at any time. She has a fine presence, a
clear manner of expressing her ideas, and holds
her listeners spell-bound with her sympathetic
eloquence.  We shall publish her speech in our
next issue of the paper, therefore we do not desire
to make any extracts, to spoil the effect. Mrs.
Thatcher was introduced to the audience by Miss
Keogh of this city in a few well-chosen words.
While here Mr. and Mrs. Thatcher were the
guests of Presiding Bishop Preston.  We had
an opportunity of meeting the distinguished lady
previous to her departure from this City, and
found her exceedingly interesting in conversation,
and well posted on all general matters pertaining
to women, as well as those directly connected 
with the World's Fair work.


INTERESTING LETTER.

	We publish extracts from a letter received
from Miss Balgarnie, dated May 19, 1891, which
we feel sure our readers will be interested in, as
well as to know that she arrived home safely
after her visit to America. Miss Balgarnie is a
most valiant young woman in the field of work in
Great Britain. While in this country she made
a tour of the United States and Canada, looking
up on many vital questions especially those per-
taining to woman’s status, and particularly wages.
Her ideas on the great questions of the day are
very practical and she knows how to express her
views clearly, as well as eloquently. We shall
ever regret her not being able to speak in this


city, for her manner is so attractive that many
opposed to the question of woman's suffrage and
kindred topics would certainly listen to her be-
cause of her eloquence, and might thus be in-
fluenced against their previous prejudices.—ED.
		*	*	*	*	*	*
	"I would have written before, but I was far
from well for a fortnight after landing, having
caught cold on the boat. I am all right now, and
in the midst of work; this week we have had the
Women's Liberal Federation meeting presided
over by Lady Aberdeen. I spoke on Woman's 
Wages, and told of what I had seen amongst
women Factory inspectors in your country—I
hope ere long we may have them here. One
has already been appointed in Nottingham, and
there are here three women acting as Sub-com-
missioners for the Royal Commission now investi-
gating the Labor question. It is very nice to find
my country women so alive, and immense strides
have been made while I have been away. In
this year the suffrage has advanced more than
during ten previous years."
	"I had a pleasant trip, the Sea was calm; but
amongst all my recollections some of the most
interesting and pleasant are of the day spent in
your hospitable city, amongst all your kind
friends. I do thank you all and you especially
for all you did to make me happy. You will be
seeing Mrs. Beattie, Mrs. Sarah M. Kimball, Mrs.
Richards and others,—please give them my very
kindest remembrances.
	I hope if you are ever here that you will come
to see me.
			*	*	*	*	*	*
				Yours Very Sincerely,
						FLORENCE BALGARNIE.


AN INTERESTING JOURNEY.

	Monday night the 11th of April, in company
with Pres. F. A. Hammond, Sister Elizabeth
Howard and Mary A. Freeze, left the City by way
of the D. and R. G. R. R., to visit San Juan
Stake in the interest of women's organizations.
Sister Howard has given a description of her
journey for the EXPONENT, she says, "We en-
joyed the delightful scenery on the train by
moonlight, rather than sleeping, reached Thomp-
son's Springs the following morning, where a
carriage was in waiting to take us to Moab, arriv-
ing at that place about 4 p. m. We held meetings
the next day at 10 a. m., with the Relief Society,
and at 2 p. m. with the Y. L. M. I. A., and re-
mained there until the next morning, when
Brother Holyoak and wife went with us on our
way having fitted up a team with every conveni-
ence for traveling. After driving about 15 miles
we stopped to rest our horses etc., and lunched
at Kane Spring's; resuming our journey we
traveled over fearful gulches and dugways finally
coming to a lovely place, very grand, we camped
for the night, made a great fire of pine trees, pre-
pared supper and after singing and prayer—we
retired, slept well in the open air and felt refresh-
ed when we rose early in the morning; break-
fasted and pursued our way; arrived about noon
at Petersen's hill where we lunched; on our trip that
day we passed between the La Salle and Blue
Mountains far, far apart, and arrived at Monticello
at five p.m. where several of the sisters of the
Relief Society came to visit us at Bishop Jones,
whose wife is President of the Relief Society at
that place.
	"Next day, 16th, held meeting with the Relief
Society, had a large attendance; after dinner,
Young Ladies meeting, the house filled with
young men and young women;—this place is
7,500 feet above the level of the sea, a large level
tract of land, they raise good wheat and have ex-
cellent fruit; this locality is a grand site for the


[Column 1]
building of a magnificent city; looking over the
country one can see an immense distance around
the horizon;here we held a meeting of the Primary
Association at 7 p. m., stayed and attended pub-
lic Sunday services, meeting at ten in the meet-
ing house, Pres. Hammond gave a graphic des-
cription of the cap stone services at S. L. City
on the 6th of April; with which the people were
greatly delighted, as were we also. Sister
Freeze and myself both addressed the congrega-
tion a short time by special request.
	"Brother Geo. Adams came well prepared and
took us to our next camping place Alkali Springs;
we had a delightful drive, passed in the distance
the Elk mountains, when between 30 and 40 miles
on our journey we saw a hundred miles in the
distance, standing alone in a vast plain, a mag-
nificent structure of stone, which resembles a
large castle called "Cathedral Rock"-it stands
1,000 feet high and is one of the most striking
pictures of that wonderful country.
	"We camped for lunch that day on the bank of
Recapture river, which has some sort of tradition
of Indians, Spanish, etc., passed Comb-Wash
Rocks, prominent and steep very noticable, this
ledge is 30 miles long; afterwards we passed
Cotton wood wash a sort of gulch twining in and
out for 40 miles-over rough rocks and down
Cow-Canon one of the grandest places I have
ever seen-it was solid rock, the road had been
blasted, and cut, and dug through here at im-
mense expense and labor;-this canon ushers us
into the beautiful valley where Bluff city is situ-
ated on the San Juan river, where we arrived safe
on the 18th at the residence of Pres. Hammond
and there met his amiable wife and lovely
daughters. Tuesday we held the Young Ladies
meeting; on Wednesday held a meeting of the
Relief Society, and the same evening an informal
meeting of the officers of the several organiza-
tions R. S., Y. L., and P. A. at Sister Josephine
Wood's which we enjoyed very much, conversing
with these good sisters who live so far away from
the centre Stake of Zion.
	"The next day Mrs. Decker, Mrs. Hammond,
and Mrs. Freeze and myself ascended a very
high and steep hill, evidently the remains of some
ancient and immense ruin; it bore every appear-
ance of it;-on the top after our climbing and
clambering on and over the rocks we were per-
fectly delighted to find, and we picked up broken
pieces of ancient pottery, interesting curiosities
probably from utensils of the ancient inhabitants;
at the extreme end of this lofty eminence, is the
cemetery for Bluff, and to reach it the road winds
round and round a long distance, though we had
climbed up the other way.
	"The same afternoon, Thursday, at 2 p. m. we
organized a Woman's Suffrage Association for
San Juan County, with an efficient corps of
officers; considerable interest was manifested on
the subject and no doubt there will be quite an
awakening among the women of the vicinity.
	"Friday at 2 p. m. a general meeting of the
brethren and sisters, at which instructions were
given in all departments of woman's work and
improvement.
	"Saturday there was a Primary meeting well at-
tended; children orderly, and a fine programme
well carried out. Sunday School on Sunday
morning where both of us spoke, and afternoon
meeting where we both addressed the congrega-
tion by invitation.
	"On Monday we left Bluff early in the morning
Sister Hammond joining us, and traveled by
several Cliff Builders dwellings,which were great
curiosities and very interesting; Sister Freeze
went into one of them, was helped up one narrow
step after another in the rocky way by Brother
Burton, until she reached the opening, and had
the opportunity of going inside through the
window as the door was inaccessible. We rest-
ed at Montezuma Wash and lunched there under

[Column 2]
some very grand old trees. We traveled over an
extraordinary formation of art called Corduroy
hill; at the foot of which there are millions of
marbles formed by the elements as hard as rocks.
We crossed Mac’EImo’s Wash, a very hazardous
exploit, then we came to a trading post where
there were a number of Indians, it is named Peak
City; that night we camped out again and indeed
the making of our beds of the pine tree boughs at
our camping places was something quite ro-
mantic
	"Tuesday morning we crossed the line into
Colorado, we lunched in camp and soon came to
the Mancos river where we had supper on the
banks, the river was so high and rapid that Pres.
Hammond was anxious to know the depth of the
water before we crossed, and called to a stalwart
young Indian on the opposite side of the stream,
who wrapping his blanket around him, strode
through the stream, and thus finding it safe to
cross we drove over, the water reaching the edge
of the carriage, but toiling up the other side was
difficult; immediately after we crossed the river
we saw large flocks of young lambs and kids,
beautiful and white, feeding and playing about,
it was a very pretty sight;these all belonged to the
squaws in that vicinity; we rode to a ravine where
we camped over night; we traveled on in the
morning and breakfasted in a grand canon, a
most romantic place, and Mrs. Freeze and I
climbed the hills and picked up more pieces of
pottery and curiosities; we arrived in Olio in the
afternoon. The next day in the afternoon we
held a meeting of the Relief Society at Brother
Geo. Burnham's, and in the evening in the meet-
ing house where there was a large assembly, not
only of our people but other denominations.
	"Next day we paid a visit to an Indian trading
post, to reach, which place we crossed the San
Juan river in a ferry boat which is a wide turbu-
lent stream; we had a very nice visit with the
trader's wife and family.
	"We also visited Mrs. Abagail Stevens and
family, and at her house we held a meeting of the
Relief Society; Saturday we held a general meet-
ing in the meeting house not only attended by our
own people but other denominations as well.
	"Sunday being the general fast day observed
throughout the several stakes of Zion, we at-
tended the meeting, there were people from La
Platta as well; at this meeting every one present
bore their testimony young and old which was a
spiritual feast indeed. In the evening of the same
day the sisters held a general meeting of all de-
nominations, and it was a very interesting time.
	"Monday we spent the time in visiting,Tuesday
morning Brother and Sister Hammond started
off early; later in the day we left in company
with Bishop Burnham; we traveled to La Platta,
the scenery was grand all the journey; traveled
by the La Platta river and reached the town of
La Platta in time to hold meeting in the evening
at Brother Roger's, had a good attendance.
	The next morning May 4, we went to Sister
Lydia Taylor's and as she had a large and
spacious room suitable to convene an assembly,
the funeral of Axel Swan was held at her house
he was a promising intelligent young man, who
had died the day previous. Our arrival was
timely for the parents were grieving inconsolably
as it were,and we were enabled to comfort them in
a measure and soften the terrible blow that
seemed to have fallen upon them so heavily. In
the afternoon we held Primary meeting and the
children acquitted themselves very creditably in-
deed. All the Primary Associations seemed to
be well regulated and the children under good
training. Our journey has been delightful in
many ways, and as we were not hurried we had
excellent opportunity to familiarize ourselves with
the people and understand things much better
than in a hasty trip.
[To be continued.]


WOMAN’S EXPONENT.


ANNIVERSARY AT PLASANT GREEN.

	The sisters of Plasant Green Relief Socity
met at the ward house on Thursday, May 19,
1892, to celebrate the anniversary of their or-
ganization. There were present on the stand
from Salt Lake City the General President
Zina D. H. Young and her Counselor Bath-
sheba Smith, and President M. I. Horne of the
Salt Lake Stake; of the ward authorities
Pres. Alvira S. Hirst, Counselor Ann C.
Bertoch, Bishop L. N. Hardman, Counselors
H. T. Spencer and John Hirst.
	Pres. Alvira S. Hirst presided. After the
usual exercises Pres. Horne addressed the
congregation in a very encouraging and in-
structive manner. Sister Smith then addressed
the assembly in a very pleasing manner, then
the beautiful Jubilee song was sung, entitled
“The Daughters of Zion, the Friends of the
Poor.” Pres. Zina Young occupied the stand
and exhorted the sisters to diligence and to
learn and know the will of our Heavenly
Father. Bishop L. N. Hardman exhorted
the sisters to faithfulness and to be diligent
in all things. Counselor H. T. Spencer ad-
dressed the sisters in a pleasing manner.
President Charlotte Nealson of Hunter ward
addressed the assembly in a brief but pleasant
manner. Counselor John Hirst exhorted the
sisters to be faithful and press forward.
Counselor Ann C. Bertoch said she was
thankful to have our beloved sisters in our
midst and asked God to strengthen us. Pres.
Alvira S. Hirst urged the sisters to treasure
up the good things that they had heard and
profit by them, for we had indeed had a time
of rejoicing and a feast of refreshing. Meeting
then adjourned.
	Another meeting was held in the after-
noon for the purpose of organizing a Primary
Association. Pres. Hirst presiding; after the
usual exercises were over, Pres. Zina Young
with the assistance of Pres. Hirst, proceeded
to organize a Primary, which was effected.
The following named officers were sustained,
Mrs. Fanny S. Brown, President; Mrs. Sarah
H. Coon and Miss Maggie Hardman Coun-
selors; Miss Maud Spencer, Secretary; Master
Lott Hancock, Cor. Sec.; Master William C.
Bertosh, Treasurer. After the organization
was completed Pres. Zina Young addressed
the little people in her pleasing motherly way.
Bishop Hardman, Counselor John Hirst, Coun-
selor Ann Bertoch, Mrs. M. J. C. Auer all
addressed the little people in a pleasing and
instructive manner. Pres. Fanny S. Brown
and Counselors expressed their willingness to
labor for the upbuilding of the Primary, God
giving them strength. Pres. Alvira Hirst
made a few closing remarks asking God to bless
us one and all, that we might be found worthy
to be numbered with his chosen sons and
daughters.
	ALVIRA S. HIRST, President.
	FANNY H. JENKINS, Secretary R. S.


SOMETHING ABOUT VOTING.

	Mrs. President, friends and members of the
Association: it has fallen to my lot to write
something for our suffrage meeting, I have en-
deavored to do my best, and I trust that it
will be interesting and benefical to my hearers;
this is not a religious assembly, but for all that
we need the Spirit of God to direct us, we as
ladies and sisters should work together in
union and strive to have the approval of our
Heavenly Father.
	Why should men fear us at the polls? is it
because they themselves are so corrupt? There
is no mother, wife, daughter, or sister, but that
will vote for an honorable man instead of those
that seek the overthrow of good men; a
mother has the interest of her children at heart


and she desires the company in which they
move to be pure and upright.
	No matter how degraded a woman may
have become herself, she wishes not only to
see her own sons and daughters virtuous, but
also those with whom they may associate.
Men do not give women the credit of being
smart enough to vote. If the mothers are
capable of rearing sons that can vote, why not
her daughters, you would not see a lady reel
up to the Ballot Box, and not know for whom 
she was voting as you will some men, and
you would not see a mother selling her vote
for liquor or a meal,a drunken father is allowed
to vote while a good mother cannot say who
shall govern her sons and daughters; why
even tramps have been allowed to vote.  For
whom do they vote?  Men of the best and
finest type? no, that was not the object of their
vote, it was to get board and lodging.  Would
you find women doing such a degrading thing?
I think not, they would do their best to get an
honest meal; you will not find them tramping,
begging for something to eat from persons that
are sober and industrious.
	Some men do not think that the wife has
time enough to vote, but I think women can
make time for that, it does not take long to
deposit a ballot, that is a very poor excuse.
She needs a rest from sweeping and dusting
and looking after domestic affairs, the mother
would not spend the same time in voting as
her husband would; after she had cast her
vote, you would see her returning to her home 
and to her family, to look after their welfare;
not going away to places for drink, or loafing
on the street cornors.
	Some tell us we are likely to be insulted if
we go to vote; why need we fear anything of
the kind? why not go arm in arm with our
husband or brother? if a woman has property
she is forced to have the time to pay her taxes
and it would not take her any longer to vote
than to pay taxes, and she meets the same
class of men at the one place, as she does at
the other.  If we are to be taxed the same as
men why are we deprived of the right of say-
ing who shall tax us? it is time we were awak-
ing, and asking for our rights; for it is our
right and not a privilege.
	Some argue that for women to vote would
cause family trouble, why should it? man
should not be selfish enough to want his wife
to vote the way he does, the wife has the same
right to use her own judgment as the husband
has; for she will lend her influence in the
right direction nine cases out of ten.
	Let us educate ourselves in these matters that
when we get our franchise we may appreciate
its full value and not abuse it.  Let us honor
it as a God-given right is the desire of one of
your number. 
					ANNIE C. HINDLEY.


WOMAN’S EXPONENT.


"WILL YE GO AWAY?"

	No, Lord we have loved thee too long and
too ardently to go away from thee!  Away
from thee?  Our best friend—our brother—
our advocate with the Father, thou who hast
put away our sins by the shedding of thy heart's
blood, who hast died to purchase for us the
bliss of heaven, in whose name alone we can be
saved?  We are constrained to say in the language
of St. Peter, "Lord, to whom shall we go? Thou
hast the words of eternal life.  And we believe
and are sure that Thou art that Christ, the
Son of the living God."
	Noble Peter—we cannot help loving him for
his boldness and courage in confessing his
Lord.  We are also drawn to him by intense
sympathy which springs from common weak-
ness.  So often we stumble and fall, that we
turn with a kind of relief to him whose steps
faltered even as ours and sometimes as sadly


went astray.  And yet there is something so
interesting and profitable in the story of Peter,
that we turn again and again to study it with
ever increasing delight.  But like us, Peter
was human and his self-reliance caused him to
fall.  This was preceded by the "following afar
off;" had he kept close to his Lord, he would
not have fallen.  Those who have reached
three score and ten, or fourscore years, can on
looking back see many times that they have fol-
lowed afar off until there has come again and
again a gentle reminder, a tender look which
has caused us to weep bitterly, and it is this
experience which forces us to look forward a
few years, with sorrow of heart, when the
young people of this community, will begin to
see the emptiness of worldly pleasure and will 
wish they had not thrown away so many gold-
en opportunities.
	Oh! ye young Latter Day Saints, many of
you have professed to receive the gospel﻿ in
your native land, you have been baptized into
the Church of Christ, you have crossed the
ocean in order to live with the Saints in the
land of Zion, but how is it now?  Many of you
prefer the sinful pleasures which are provided
by the Gentile world on the Sabbath for your
overthrow.
	Others prefer novel-reading to meeting with
the Saints to listen to the instructions of God's
servants.  Does reading a novel increase your
relish for the Bible, Book of Mormon, or any
other sacred book? Does it help you to culti-
vate the spirit of prayer, while your seat in
God's house is vacant? No, it does not.  A
novel reader is seldom a reader of sacred books.
Many and various are the traps set by the
world for your unwary feet, and when you fall
into them, they say in their hearts, “Ah! so
would we have it.” Then comes the sting for
every sinful pleasure is provided with a sting,
ready to pierce you as soon as the gratification
is over. Well will it be for you if the Lord
should give you such a look as He did Peter,
which will cause you to weep biterly. Re-
member you will be called togive an account of
every idle word and every unholy act in the
day of judgment. Let us take the advice of
holy writ which says—“take with you words,
and turn to the Lord, say unto Him, take
away all iniquity, and receive us graciously;”
and He will do it,but His Spirit will not strive
with us for ever. Your Redeemer is asking
you in tender pity, “will ye also go away?”
Oh! let your answer be,“Lord to whom shall we
go?  Thou hast the words of eternal life.”
					MARY Y. CORBY.


WOMAN’S NATIONAL COUNCIL.

Editor Woman’s Journal:
	The annual business meeting of the Nation-
al Council of Women was held at Chicago,
May 9, 10.
	A report from the Committee on Equal
Wages for Equal Work, from its Chairman,
Mrs. Harriet Taylor Upton, was read by the
Corresponding Secretary. It reported progress
in the work of presenting to and urging upon
Congress a bill to secure to government em-
ployees equal wages for equal work, regardless
of sex.
	The President and Secretary were directed
by the meeting to send to the General Confer-
ence of the M. E. Church in session at Omaha,
Neb., in the care of Bishop Fitzgerald, a let-
ter on the admission of women as members of
the Conference. The following letter, signed
by these two officers, was sent:

To the General Conference of the M. E. Church,
	Care Bishop Fitzgerald, Omaha, Nebraska.

	Honored Friends:—The National Council
of Women of the United States, representing
at its last annual meeting sixty-two separate
national societies of women, in all depart-

[Column 2]
ments of beneficent effort and from all parts
of the nation, respectfully congratulate the
General Conference of the great church repre-
sented by your assembly on the majority vote
of ministers and laity in favor of the equal
participation of women in the councils of the
church wherein they form two-thirds of the
membership. We believe this action repre-
sents the most intelligent public opinion of the
present  time, and we, as women workers,
await with earnest expectation the forward
movement in one of the world’s greatest
churches as the forerunner of such universal
action on the part of all churches as shall
realize the New Testament declaration, "There
is neither male nor female, but ye are all one
in Christ Jesus.”
	On behalf of the National Council of Wo-
men of the United States,
	Yours, with high esteem,
		MAY WRIGHT SEWALL, President.
		RACHEL FOSTER AVERY, Cor. Sec.

	The Corresponding Secretary was directed
to write to the Secretary of the National Di-
vorce Reform League, Dr. Samuel Dike,
Auburndale, Mass., stating the desire of the
Council that women be placed upon the Board,
and that the Council has already appointed a
committee to urge this point with the League
	An interesting discussion was called out by
the report of the Dress Reform Committee.
It was decided to have the Symposium on
Dress, which has been prepared by this Com-
mittee for the Council, appear in the Arena.
	The following resolution, offered by Mrs.
E. B. Grannis, President of the National So-
ciety for the Promotion of Social Purity, was
unanimously adopted:

	Resolved, That it is the sense of the Execu-
tive Council of Women of the United States,
that the Health Boards of Hungary and Aus-
tria have set a true key-note for the dress-
reform by urging that legislation be had to
prevent the wearing of long skirts upon the
street, in view of the fact that such skirts gath-
er up poisonous material from the filth of the
streets, and thus endanger the public health.

	The President of the National Council, Mrs.
May Wright Sewall, gave a most encouraging
report of the progress of the Council movement
in foreign countries. As a result of the great
meeting of the French women held in 1889,
the inspiration to which was given by the In-
ternational Council at Washington in 1888, a
National Council of the Women of France
has been formed, and is ready to act in con-
junction with the National Council of the
United States, through the International
Council of Women. In Finland the
national bodies of women are federated through
the effects of Baroness Alexandra Gripenberg,
the representative of that country in the In-
ternational Council of 1888. The three Scan-
dinavian countries, Norway, Sweden and Den-
mark, have lately formed a Scandinavian
Council of Women. In Italy, a sort of Na-
tional Club of women has been formed, with
Queen Margherita as its leader and chief of-
ficer.
	Mrs. M. R. M. Wallace, President of the
Illinois Industrial Reform School for Girls,
member of the National Council, informed the
meeting of a convention of Industrial Reform
Schools which was held in Chicago, May 25.
Mrs. Wallace expects the result of this meet-
ing to be a national association of such schools.
In case of the formation of such an association,
if it shall enter the Council, Mrs. Wallace
thinks the school of which she is a representa-
tive would yield its place to the national society
representing the same idea.
	On Tuesday morning, Mrs. Frank Stuart
Parker, a member of the Council’s Committee
on Dress, gave some interesting information
on the subject, spoke of the large Dress Re-


form Association existing in Chicago, of the
approaching publication of a valuable work
on artistic dress, and promised that her own
contribution to the Symposium would be ready
within a week. Mrs. Parker said that she
could get photographs of some of the dresses
which would be described in her article. She
was instructed to do so, that they may be used
to illustrate the Symposium. On motion of
Miss Willard the resolution,on dress passed at
Washington, D. C., was passed again by the
Executive Meeting, with some slight changes.
	A discussion of headquarters followed. Mrs.
M. R. M. Wallace, Miss Anthony, Miss Wil-
lard and the President urged the importance
of permanent headquarters on the Fair
Grounds, and suggested the advisability of
other headquarters in the city before the
dedicatory exercises of the Exposition in Oc-
tober, 1892. It was decided that the Council,
having been invited by Mrs. Palmer, President
of the Board of Lady Managers, to make its
headquarters in the Woman’s Building, pre-
pare to occupy said headquarters during the
entire time of the Exposition. The Secretary
was directed to notify all national organizations
of women not already members of the Council
that they are invited to enter it, and that all
its members shall have desk room in its head-
quarters at the World’s Fair.
	Miss Anthony and Mrs. Avery reported
their informal talk with Mr. Bonney, President
of the Auxiliary Congresses of the Columbian
Exposition, and his expression of welcome to
the Council of Women; also that he thought
it would be possible to arrange that the Coun-
cil should have free use of the auditoriums
and some committee rooms of the Art Palace,
where the Congresses are to be held, yet re-
taining the entire management of its own meet-
ings. It was resolved that the application of
the Council be sent to President Bonney for
the gratuitous use of two audience rooms and
five committee rooms, day and evening, for one
week, and that the Council ask for such use of
the Art Palace for one week immediately pre-
ceding the meeting of the World’s Woman’s
Temperance Union.
	Tuesday afternoon it was decided, on mo-
tion of Miss Willard, that the Council provide
for a list of “Patrons of the Council.” Any
person whose name is acceptable to the gen-
eral officers may become a patron by payment
of $100 to the Council.
	The resignation of Mrs. Ella Dietz Clymer
from the position of vice-president of the Coun-
cil, on account of poor health, was accepted
with regret.
	The following resolution was passed:

	Resolved, That we commend to the general
officers of the Council, with power to act, the
desirability of carrying out the plan proposed
by the first President in her address at Wash-
ington, D. C., in 1891, in regard to holding
a legislative congress of women at the Nation-
al Capital during the session of Congress, to
act on measures touching the welfare of wo-
men,and recommend the same to the consider-
ation of Congress.

	The President announced her intention of
spending the summer in Europe working in
the interests of the Council, and received the
unanimous approval of the meeting upon her
plan of dividing her time between England
and Germany.
			RACHEL FOSTER AVERY,
					Cor. Sec. N. C. W.


WOMANS EXPONENT.


COMPENSATION.

	[Extract from an essay by Ralph Waldo
Emerson.]
	And yet the compensations of calamity are
made apparent to the understanding also, after


long intervals of time.  A fever, a mutilation,
a cruel disappointment, a loss of wealth, a loss
of friends,seems at the moment unparalleled loss
and unpayable.  But the sure years reveal the
deep remedial force that underlies all facts.
The death of a dear friend, wife, brother, lover,
which seemed nothing but privation, somewhat
later assumes the aspect of a guide, or genius;
for it commonly operates revolutions in our
way of life, terminates an epoch of infancy, or
of youth,  which was waiting to be closed,
breaks up a wonted occupation, or a house-
hold, or style of living, and allows the for-
mation of new ones, more friendly to the
growth of character. It permits or constrains
the formation of new acquaintances, and the
reception of new influences, that prove of the
first importance to the next years; and the
man or woman who would have remained a
sunny, garden flower, with no room for its
roots and too much sunshine for its head, by
the falling of the walls and the neglect of
the gardener, is made the banian of the for-
est, yielding shade and fruit to wide neighbor-
hoods of men.”


GLEANINGS.

	Culture does not exist for its own sake, but
for the sake of added power in all the relations
of life.—Mrs. Charlotte Emerson Brown, New
Jersey.

	Every woman’s club is a continual post-
graduate course for every woman in it, and
for the husbands at home as well.—Mrs. May
Wright Sewall, Indianapolis.

	Literary clubs must be progressive, but not
aggressive.—Mrs. Etta S. Osgood, Portland, Me.

	The object of the woman’s club is to make
every woman’s life more useful to her family
and sweeter to herself.—Mrs. Mary Jameson
Judah, Memphis, Tenn.

	God’s truths always make enthusiasts when
properly understood.—Mrs. J. M. Crouse,
Chicago, Ill.

	Man has been so afraid for his rights that
he has lost the greatest of his rights—the in-
telligent help of woman.—Dr. Sarah Hackett
Stevenson, Chicago.


Margaret Manton Merrill is meeting with
great success, reciting in London. She has
made a hit in George Sutton’s "Pint of Fizz."
Mrs. Merrill, it will be remembered, has done
excellent newspaper work on the New York
Herald.
	English women are very active in politics.
The Women’s Liberal Society, at the head
of which is Mrs. Gladstone, numbers now fully
100,000 members, and the Primrose League,
which is an older organization, made famous
by such women as Lady Randolph Churchill,
is even larger.—Ex.


OBITUARIES.

	Sarah Mulim Hill Mc Kendrick, born April
1st, 1862 in Ogden, Utah, died March 24,
1892 in Wellington, Utah.  She leaves a
family of small children, the youngest nine
days old; she was a faithful member of the
Relief, Society.
							COM.

	At Round Valley, Gila Co.  Arizona,
on May 5, 1892, the Death Angel carried
from our midst Lenore Gibson, aged 2 1/2 years,
daughter of Joseph and Ruth Gibson. She
had been a cripple since Sept. last, but was
improving slowly, when she was taken with


inflammation of the stomach. On May fifth
she was carried across that “Silvery Water,”
where she will await the coming of her loved
ones. No pure or more patient child ever liv-
ed. Too good and pure to live in this sinful
world.
						COM.
	The Lord in His infinite wisdom has seen fit
to call from our midst on the 10th of April
1892, our beloved Sister Maria Stevenson,
wife of William Richardson, aged 65 years.
	The deceased was born at Doncaster, Eng-
land. Was baptised into the Church of Jesus
Christ of Latter day Saints in 1847. Emigra-
ted to Utah in 1888. Came with her husband
to Plain City, and stayed with her nephew
Bishop J. W. Bramwell until they bought a
home, where they have lived until her death.
She was always patient in her sickness, suffered
much from liver and kidney disease which
caused her death. She was an affectionate and
loving wife and mother, a true and noble Lat-
terday Saint, for whose loss her bereaved hus-
band and her devoted sister M. A. Bramwell
with a host of loving nephews and nieces, and
her sisters of the Relief Society all are left to
mourn; but with the assurance of a glorious
resurrection.
		M. A. W. SPIERS, Sec. R. S.
	[Millennial Star please copy.]

	Our beloved sister M. A. Humphry Miller,
beloved wife of Brother Stephen Miller, died
at her residence in Plain City on the 1st of
April, 1892. Deceased was born in Heathfield,
Sussex Co., England in 1812, was baptized
into the Church of Jesus Christ of Latter-day
Saints in Nov. 1854. Emigrated to Utah with
her husband and family in the year 1862; lived
in Wellsville Cache Valley four years, moved
to Plain City in 1866 where she has lived un-
til her death.
	Dear Sister Miller was a great sufferer all
through the last winter from a complication
of complaints, sciatica lung and heart disease,
terminating in pneumonia. She was a faith-
ful member of the Relief Society for many
years, and was an earnest worker as a teach-
er in the south district for a long time.
	She leaves a most loving husband, two sons,
and one daugh er to mourn her loss. Dear
Sister Miller was a true Latter-day Saint, an
affectionate wife and mother; in all her fearful
suffering she was never heard to complain.
One singular thing in her experience is that
in 1862, when she crossed the plains, her
health was so bad that all thought she would
die, and that she was too weak to bear the
journey in her then delicate condition, yet so
great was her faith in the power of healing,
that she was better in her confinement than
with any of her former children; although she
was then over 50 years of age.
	Rest, dear sister, in peace!
	M. A. W. SPIERS Sec. 	R. S. Plain City.
